gaps.description: The Bill of Rights (BoR) is an empty scaffold with all articles undefined, yet the DIO is actively performing state changes (creating entities, running autonomous processes, provisioning projects). The BoR is supposed to define the organism's constitutional identity, scope of enterprise, prohibited activities, and human authority reservations. Without it, the DIO operates with no constitutional boundaries, rendering the MSP subordinate to nothing and the entire governance structure foundationless.

The MSP is a seed document with no rules, and its amendment protocol creates a deadlock: amendments originate only from Policy Exception Messages (PEMs) issued when an Action Proposal is blocked by MSP/genome constraints. Because no rules exist, no AP can be blocked, no PEM can be generated, and the Senate cannot ever publish substantive rules. The DIO is therefore trapped in default-permissive mode indefinitely, contradicting the stated purpose of the MSP as an operational constitution that translates the BoR into machine-actionable constraints.

Governance organs (Nomos, Senate, Arbiter, etc.) are all exhibiting rapid state oscillation between DEGRADED and ALIVE, as shown by continuous spine transitions. This instability casts doubt on the reliability of the governance pipeline even if the BoR were completed and the MSP deadlock resolved. The MSP presumes these organs are operational for AP evaluation, PEM issuance, and amendment handling.

raw_responses:
{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "id": "gap-bor-001",
      "description": "The Bill of Rights has all articles undefined ('scaffold — awaiting human authorship'). The Arbiter scope gate cannot enforce any constitutional perimeter; any action proposal would pass the scope check by default. This leaves the DIO without legal boundaries for scope of enterprise, prohibited activities, core identity, human authority reservations, amendment protocol, or interpretation rules.",
      "grounding_msp_section": null,
      "grounding_bor_article": "Articles I-VI (all scaffold — awaiting human authorship)",
      "criticality": 10,
      "urgency": 10,
      "impact": 10,
      "priority_score": 1000
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-msp-001",
      "description": "The MSP Rules section is empty. No operational invariants, constraints, SLAs, organ availability targets, runtime targets, or cadence baselines are defined. The DIO operates under default-permissive evaluation, meaning all actions are allowed. The observed spine transitions (mass simultaneous degradation) cannot be assessed against any operational standard, and no automated corrective response exists.",
      "grounding_msp_section": "MSP — Rules (empty)",
      "grounding_bor_article": null,
      "criticality": 9,
      "urgency": 9,
      "impact": 9,
      "priority_score": 729
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-msp-002",
      "description": "Spine-state transitions show a repeating pattern: all organs simultaneously transition to DEGRADED due to 'missed_pong' and then recover to ALIVE within seconds ('pong_recovered'). This indicates a lack of resilience mechanisms, such as separate health channels, staggered degradation thresholds, or retry policies. The absence of such operational invariants (which the MSP Purpose envisions but no rules currently specify) means a single network glitch can degrade the entire organ fleet, creating a high-risk single point of failure.",
      "grounding_msp_section": "MSP — Purpose (translates BoR into precise, machine-actionable set of objectives, invariants, constraints, and safety rules)",
      "grounding_bor_article": null,
      "criticality": 8,
      "urgency": 8,
      "impact": 8,
      "priority_score": 512
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-msp-003",
      "description": "No health-check cadence baselines are defined. The spine logs show a missed_pong triggering immediate DEGRADED for an organ, and a single pong restoring ALIVE. Without explicit thresholds (e.g., consecutive miss count, grace period, recovery stabilization time), the system exhibits high-frequency flapping (organs flip state within ~30-second windows). This flapping can cause instability, excessive logging, and potential race conditions. A cadence baseline rule is required to govern ping intervals, failure detection time, and recovery validation.",
      "grounding_msp_section": "MSP — Rules (none), but mission frame calls for 'cadence baselines'",
      "grounding_bor_article": null,
      "criticality": 7,
      "urgency": 7,
      "impact": 7,
      "priority_score": 343
    }
  ]
}
